clinical_history: 26 year-old female with complaint of palpitations. The ECG shows all of the following findings, EXCEPT?
diagnosis:
This ECG shows sinus bradycardia at about 58 bpm, with frequent premature ventricular premature beats (VPBs) in a bigeminal pattern. Sinus beats have right axis deviation with a borderline intraventricular conduction delay (QRS=110 msec). The precordial leads have prominent voltage with possible left ventricular hypertrophy (deep S wave in V2) and the rightward axis also raises consideration of biventricular hypertrophy. (Young adults may have prominent precordial voltage as a physiologic finding.) 

The VPBs have right bundle branch block morphology with a superior-rightward axis consistent with an origin in the apicolateral portion of the left ventricle.

Aberrated APBs are excluded as there are no ectopic P waves seen prior to the QRS of the premature beat. The sinus P waves actually march through (partly obscured at times), giving a classic compensatory pause pattern characteristic of VPBs.

This patient had an idiopathic cardiomyopathy and episodes of ventricular tachycardia from a site or sites in the apical left ventricle not amenable to ablation therapy.
